name: RepAndyKim
tweet: Together, these two bills tonight are about changing systems: our system of politics and our system of criminal justice. We can only do better for everyone when the systems are built to allow us to do that. Today was an important step in that direction (end)

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: This was a key part of what I heard when I did a series of listening sessions last year with community groups and law enforcement. There was a lot of common ground and common purpose. This bill is a step towards achieving that.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: This bill achieves that by banning practices like chokeholds and taking other steps to change the culture of our public safety so it works for everyone.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: Best practices, accountability, bringing our police and our communities closer together make for better, safer communities. That's our goal.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: This bill is actually the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act. It's named after Mr. Floyd because he serves as a reminder of a broken system. Broken for communities of color and broken for our men and women in uniform who serve and protect.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: That means we have a system that can truly tackle the big challenges of our time. One of those challenges is racial justice. That's where the second bill, the #JusticeInPolicingAct comes in.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: When our system doesn't work for you, it doesn't work. And when our system doesn't work, it doesn't work for you. We've seen that so many times over the past few years. This bill makes the changes we need to make the system work, and work for you.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: This bill does a lot of things, but it comes down to a few key categories: 1) it protects every American's right to vote and makes our voting systems safer, 2) it finally fights back against dark money, and 3) it strengthens our ethics laws to weed out bad actors.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: So how do we fix this? We need more testing, we need guarantees that results are processed rapidly, we need to be able to surge testing capacities to hotspots as we see spikes, and we need to follow up with major investments in tracing and isolating.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: Testing is a tool. The goal isn’t to test – the goal is to get data so you can act on it. Right now, we’re failing to act because we’re not taking the data we need to act seriously.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: If you’re forced to wait a week to get results, it makes contact tracing harder, which makes containment harder.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: So what about the claim that we’re testing more than before? Yes, that’s the case. But in too many places across the country, it takes a week or more to get those test results. At that point, those tests don’t have the impact they would if you got results within 24 hours.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: His obsession only with the total number of positive cases misses three major benefits of testing: to provide proper care to those that test positive, to see where the surges are, and to ensure we try to isolate/treat those infected and contact trace.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: Why? Because following recommendations from public health leaders brings down cases, not tests. If you have a rodent problem, you don’t solve it by turning the lights out. You solve it by finding out where the mice are coming from and sealing those areas off.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: In April, rate of positive tests in my state of NJ was around 50%. We were running fewer than 7k tests per day. We’ve completed almost triple that per day now, but the number of positives is much lower than in April.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: Therefore halving the tests then halves the number of positive cases. In that line of thinking, doubling testing doubles the number of positive cases.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: First, let’s look at why it’s wrong. An important flaw of his logic is that it assumes that positivity rates for infections are constant across all levels of testing and throughout our country.

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: Okay, we know this is wrong. I’ve said that before. But since he keeps saying this, let’s dig into why it’s wrong and what it means. (thread)

name: RepAndyKim
tweet: This isn't how the math works. It's not how testing works. This isn't going to reopen our businesses and get our kids back to school.
